title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
This is the first post in a Quarto blog. Welcome!

Since this post doesn’t specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.

title: Pixels & Principles
text:
Computer Vision · Deep Learning · Research
  Pixels & Principles
  First principles for computer vision. Paper-to-code walkthroughs.Stories that make concepts stick — by Rahul Dev Singh.




  
    Series A
    First Principles
    The things nobody explained but everyone assumes you know. Images, pixels, convolutions, normalisation — done properly.
    
      1 post
      →
    
  

  
    Series B

title: About
text:
Hungry for Teaching.
I have spent years watching students confidently use big words — convolution, embedding, gradient descent — without truly understanding what is happening underneath. This blog exists to fix that.
Every post starts with a story. Sometimes from history. Sometimes from my own embarrassing past. The concept arrives last, after the story has already landed in your memory. By the time we get to the technical part, you already have a hook to hang it on.


First Principles — the things everyone assumes you know but nobody ever properly explained. One concept per post, done deeply.
Paper → Code — research papers that changed computer vision, implemented clearly from scratch. No MATLAB. No “see the official repo.” Just clean, readable Python with explanation.




dev.to/rdx099 — cross-posted articles
github.com/RDx099 — code repositories for every post
x.com/RDx099 — occasional thoughts

title: What is a Picture?
text:
When I got my first smartphone, I became obsessed with photography. I would click pictures of everything — chai, sunsets, my neighbour’s cat. One day my younger brother asked me, “Bhaiya, what actually is a photo? Like, what is it made of?”
I said, “Arrey, it is light captured on a sensor.”
He said, “Okay but what does the phone store? It does not store light.”
I had no answer. I had been taking photos for two years and I had no idea what the phone was actually saving. I just knew it looked nice on Instagram.
Years later when I started working in computer vision I realised — most people who write OpenCV code are exactly like me that day. They know it looks nice. They have no idea what it actually is.
So let us fix that. Right now.

title: What is a Picture?
text:
When I got my first smartphone, I became obsessed with photography. I would click pictures of everything — chai, sunsets, my neighbour’s cat. One day my younger brother asked me, “Bhaiya, what actually is a photo? Like what is it made of?”

title: What is a Picture?
section: What a computer sees when it looks at an image
text:
What a computer sees when it looks at an image
You look at a photo and you see a mountain, a face, a cup of chai.
The computer sees none of that. It sees a grid of numbers.
That is it. An image to a computer is nothing more than a rectangular grid of numbers. Each number represents how bright or what colour one tiny dot is. That tiny dot is called a pixel — short for picture element.
Let’s make this concrete with code.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Create a tiny 5x5 grayscale image manually
tiny_image = np.array([
    [0,   0,   0,   0,   0  ],
    [0,   128, 128, 128, 0  ],
    [0,   128, 255, 128, 0  ],
    [0,   128, 128, 128, 0  ],
    [0,   0,   0,   0,   0  ]
], dtype=np.uint8)

plt.figure(figsize=(4, 4))
plt.imshow(tiny_image, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=255)
plt.title("A 5×5 image")
plt.axis('off')
plt.show()

print("Shape:", tiny_image.shape)
print("Data type:", tiny_image.dtype)
print("Min value:", tiny_image.min(), "| Max value:", tiny_image.max())
Run this and you will see a tiny dark square with a slightly brighter centre. That is your image. Those numbers are your image. The grid of numbers IS the image — there is nothing else.

title: What is a Picture?
section: The meaning of those numbers
text:
The meaning of those numbers
In a grayscale image, each pixel is a single number between 0 and 255.

0 means completely black
255 means completely white
Everything in between is a shade of grey

Why 255? Because a pixel value is stored in 8 bits (1 byte). The maximum value 8 bits can hold is 2⁸ - 1 = 255. This is not a coincidence or an arbitrary choice — it is a direct consequence of how computers store data.
# Let us verify this
import numpy as np

pixel_value = np.uint8(200)
print(f"Pixel value: {pixel_value}")
print(f"In binary: {bin(pixel_value)}")
print(f"Bits used: 8")
print(f"Max possible: {np.iinfo(np.uint8).max}")






Note



Why does this matter? When you normalise your image for a neural network by dividing by 255, you now know exactly why. You are mapping the range [0, 255] to [0.0, 1.0]. Every value becomes a fraction of the maximum possible brightness.

title: What is a Picture?
section: Colour images — three grids, not one
text:
Colour images — three grids, not one
A grayscale image is one grid of numbers. A colour image is three grids stacked together.
Each grid is called a channel. The three channels represent:

R — how much Red is in each pixel
G — how much Green is in each pixel
B — how much Blue is in each pixel

Together, RGB can represent approximately 16.7 million colours (256 × 256 × 256).
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load a colour image
from PIL import Image
import requests
from io import BytesIO

# Create a simple colour image manually to see the channels clearly
colour_image = np.zeros((100, 100, 3), dtype=np.uint8)
colour_image[:, :, 0] = 200   # Red channel — strong
colour_image[:, :, 1] = 80    # Green channel — medium  
colour_image[:, :, 2] = 20    # Blue channel — weak

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(14, 4))

axes[0].imshow(colour_image)
axes[0].set_title("Original colour")
axes[0].axis('off')

channel_names = ['Red channel', 'Green channel', 'Blue channel']
cmaps = ['Reds', 'Greens', 'Blues']

for i in range(3):
    axes[i+1].imshow(colour_image[:, :, i], cmap=cmaps[i])
    axes[i+1].set_title(channel_names[i])
    axes[i+1].axis('off')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Shape of colour image:", colour_image.shape)
# Output: (100, 100, 3)
# Height=100, Width=100, Channels=3
Notice the shape — (100, 100, 3). This is the most important thing to understand about images in Python: height comes first, then width, then channels. Not width first. Height first. This trips up almost every beginner when they start working with image dimensions.

title: What is a Picture?
section: What the shape tells you
text:
What the shape tells you
import cv2

img = cv2.imread('your_image.jpg')
print(img.shape)
# (480, 640, 3)
#   ^    ^   ^
#   |    |   channels (3 = colour, 1 = grayscale)
#   |    width in pixels
#   height in pixels
Every time you load an image, read its shape first. Make it a habit. The shape tells you everything: how big the image is, whether it is colour or grayscale, and how much memory it will consume.
Memory consumed by one image = height × width × channels × bytes per pixel
For a standard colour image: 480 × 640 × 3 × 1 byte = ~921,600 bytes ≈ 0.9 MB

title: What is a Picture?
section: One important warning about OpenCV
text:
One important warning about OpenCV
If you use OpenCV to load images, there is one thing that will confuse you every single time until you know about it:
OpenCV loads images in BGR order, not RGB.
import cv2
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

img_bgr = cv2.imread('your_image.jpg')   # OpenCV — loads as BGR
img_rgb = cv2.cvtColor(img_bgr, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)  # Convert to RGB

# If you show img_bgr with matplotlib — colours will look wrong
# If you show img_rgb with matplotlib — colours will look correct

plt.imshow(img_bgr)   # Wrong colours — blue and red are swapped
plt.show()

plt.imshow(img_rgb)   # Correct colours
plt.show()
This single fact causes more confusion in beginner CV code than almost anything else. Red and blue channels are swapped. OpenCV does it for historical reasons. Just remember: OpenCV = BGR. Everything else = RGB.

title: What is a Picture?
section: What you should now be able to say
text:
What you should now be able to say
Before this post, you knew an image was “a file you open with OpenCV.”
Now you know:

An image is a rectangular grid of numbers
Each number is a pixel value between 0 and 255
Grayscale images have 1 channel, colour images have 3
The shape of an image array is (height, width, channels)
OpenCV loads in BGR, not RGB — always convert before displaying

This is the foundation. Everything in computer vision — convolutions, feature detection, neural networks — operates on this grid of numbers. The better you understand the grid, the better you will understand everything built on top of it.

Next in First Principles → Why Does Color Space Matter? — RGB, HSV, LAB, and when using the wrong one silently ruins your results.

text:
When I got my first smartphone, I became obsessed with photography. I would click pictures of everything — chai, sunsets, my neighbour’s cat. One day my younger brother asked me, “Bhaiya, what actually is a photo? Like, what is it made of?”
I said, “Arrey, it is light captured on a sensor.”
He said, “Okay but what does the phone store? It does not store light.”
I had no answer. I had been taking photos for two years and I had no idea what the phone was actually saving. I just knew it looked nice on Instagram.
Years later when I started working in computer vision I realised — most people who write OpenCV code are exactly like me that day. They know it looks nice. They have no idea what it actually is.
So let us fix that. Right now.

title: What is a Picture?
section: What you see vs what the computer sees
text:
What you see vs what the computer sees
You look at a photo and you see a mountain, a face, a cup of chai.



A cup of chai — something you recognise instantly


The computer sees none of that. It sees a grid of numbers. That is it. Nothing more.
Each tiny dot in that grid is called a pixel — short for picture element. And each pixel is just a number — representing how bright or what colour that dot is.
Here is the same cup of chai, shown the way a computer actually sees it — as raw numbers:



A tiny 8×8 patch from the image — each number is one pixel's brightness (0=black, 255=white)

That table above — those are actual brightness values. That is what the computer is working with when it “looks” at your chai photo. No shapes. No objects. No meaning. Just numbers.

title: What is a Picture?
section: The meaning of those numbers
text:
The meaning of those numbers
In a grayscale image, each pixel is a single number between 0 and 255.

0 means completely black
255 means completely white
Everything in between is a shade of grey

Why 255? Because a pixel value is stored in 8 bits (1 byte). The maximum value 8 bits can hold is 2⁸ − 1 = 255. This is not arbitrary — it is a direct consequence of how computers store data.
import numpy as np

pixel_value = np.uint8(200)
print(f"Pixel value:   {pixel_value}")
print(f"In binary:     {bin(pixel_value)}")
print(f"Max possible:  {np.iinfo(np.uint8).max}")
Output:
Pixel value:   200
In binary:     0b11001000
Max possible:  255






Note



Why does this matter? When you normalise your image for a neural network by dividing by 255, you now know exactly why. You are mapping the range [0, 255] to [0.0, 1.0]. Every value becomes a fraction of the maximum possible brightness.

title: What is a Picture?
section: Creating and reading an image as numbers
text:
Creating and reading an image as numbers
Let us make this concrete. We will create a tiny 5×5 image entirely from scratch using just numbers — no camera, no file.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Create a tiny 5x5 grayscale image manually — just numbers
tiny_image = np.array([
    [  0,   0,   0,   0,   0],
    [  0, 128, 128, 128,   0],
    [  0, 128, 255, 128,   0],
    [  0, 128, 128, 128,   0],
    [  0,   0,   0,   0,   0]
], dtype=np.uint8)

print("The image IS these numbers:")
print(tiny_image)
print("\nShape:", tiny_image.shape)
Output:
The image IS these numbers:
[[  0   0   0   0   0]
 [  0 128 128 128   0]
 [  0 128 255 128   0]
 [  0 128 128 128   0]
 [  0   0   0   0   0]]

Shape: (5, 5)
# Now display it — matplotlib turns those numbers into a visible image
plt.figure(figsize=(3, 3))
plt.imshow(tiny_image, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=255)
plt.title("Our 5×5 image")
plt.axis('off')
plt.show()
Output:



A dark 5×5 square with a bright centre pixel — created entirely from numbers


You are looking at a dark square with a bright centre. That image does not come from a camera. It comes from the array of numbers we typed. The grid of numbers is the image — there is nothing else behind it.

title: What is a Picture?
section: Colour images — three grids, not one
text:
Colour images — three grids, not one
A grayscale image is one grid of numbers. A colour image is three grids stacked together.
Each grid is called a channel. The three channels in a standard colour image represent:

R — how much Red is in each pixel (0 to 255)
G — how much Green is in each pixel (0 to 255)
B — how much Blue is in each pixel (0 to 255)

Together, RGB can represent approximately 16.7 million colours (256 × 256 × 256).
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Create a 100x100 colour image — manually set each channel
colour_image = np.zeros((100, 100, 3), dtype=np.uint8)
colour_image[:, :, 0] = 200   # Red channel   — strong
colour_image[:, :, 1] = 80    # Green channel — medium
colour_image[:, :, 2] = 20    # Blue channel  — weak

print("Shape:", colour_image.shape)
print("Red channel — sample values:", colour_image[0, :5, 0])
print("Green channel — sample values:", colour_image[0, :5, 1])
print("Blue channel — sample values:", colour_image[0, :5, 2])
Output:
Shape: (100, 100, 3)
Red channel — sample values:   [200 200 200 200 200]
Green channel — sample values: [ 80  80  80  80  80]
Blue channel — sample values:  [ 20  20  20  20  20]
# Plot the image and its three channels separately
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(14, 4))

axes[0].imshow(colour_image)
axes[0].set_title("Original colour")
axes[0].axis('off')

names = ['Red channel', 'Green channel', 'Blue channel']
cmaps = ['Reds', 'Greens', 'Blues']
for i in range(3):
    axes[i+1].imshow(colour_image[:, :, i], cmap=cmaps[i], vmin=0, vmax=255)
    axes[i+1].set_title(names[i])
    axes[i+1].axis('off')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Output:



The colour image split into its three individual channels — R, G, and B


Notice the shape — (100, 100, 3). This tells you everything: height 100, width 100, and 3 channels. That third dimension is where colour lives.






Important



Channels are not always 3. In the pixel explorer above you saw 3 channels — R, G, and B. But this number changes depending on the colour scheme used. An image can have 1 channel (Grayscale), 3 channels (RGB, BGR, or HSV), or 4 channels (RGBA — where the 4th channel stores transparency, like a PNG with a transparent background). We cover all of this properly in the next post.

title: What is a Picture?
section: What the shape tells you
text:
What the shape tells you
The shape of an image array is the single most important thing to check every time you load an image.
import cv2

img = cv2.imread('your_image.jpg')
print(img.shape)
# (480, 640, 3)
#    ^    ^   ^
#    |    |   +-- channels  (3 = colour,  1 = grayscale)
#    |    +------- width in pixels
#    +------------- height in pixels
Output:
(480, 640, 3)
Memory consumed by one image = height × width × channels × bytes per pixel
For a standard colour image: 480 × 640 × 3 × 1 byte = 921,600 bytes ≈ 0.9 MB
This is why batches of images consume so much memory during training. A batch of 32 images at 224×224 pixels = 32 × 224 × 224 × 3 = 48,365,568 bytes ≈ 46 MB. Per batch. Just for the images alone.

title: What is a Picture?
section: One important warning about OpenCV
text:
One important warning about OpenCV
OpenCV loads images in BGR order, not RGB.
import cv2
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

img_bgr = cv2.imread('your_image.jpg')      # OpenCV loads as BGR
img_rgb = cv2.cvtColor(img_bgr, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)  # convert to RGB

# Displayed with matplotlib — wrong colours (blue and red swapped)
plt.imshow(img_bgr)
plt.title("BGR — colours look wrong")
plt.show()

# Correct colours
plt.imshow(img_rgb)
plt.title("RGB — correct")
plt.show()
Output:



Same image displayed as BGR (wrong colours) and RGB (correct colours)


Blue and red channels are swapped. OpenCV does this for historical reasons. Just remember the rule — OpenCV = BGR. Everything else = RGB. Always convert before displaying.

title: What is a Picture?
section: What you should now be able to say
text:
What you should now be able to say
Before this post, you knew an image was “a file you open with OpenCV.”
Now you know:

An image is a rectangular grid of numbers — nothing more
Each number is a pixel value between 0 and 255
Grayscale images have 1 channel, colour images have 3 (or sometimes 4)
The shape of an image array is (height, width, channels) — height always first
OpenCV loads in BGR, not RGB — always convert before displaying
Memory = height × width × channels — and this adds up fast

This is the foundation. Every algorithm in computer vision — convolutions, feature detection, object detection, segmentation — operates on this grid of numbers. The better you understand the grid, the better you will understand everything built on top of it.

Next in First Principles → Why Does Color Space Matter? — RGB, BGR, HSV, LAB, Grayscale — what each one actually represents and when using the wrong one silently ruins your results.

    [  0,   0,   0,   0,   0],
    [  0, 128, 128, 128,   0],

], dtype=np.uint8)

print("The image IS these numbers:")
print(tiny_image)
print("\nShape:", tiny_image.shape)
Output:
The image IS these numbers:
[[  0   0   0   0   0]
 [  0 128 128 128   0]
 [  0 128 255 128   0]
 [  0 128 128 128   0]
 [  0   0   0   0   0]]

Shape: (5, 5)
# Now display it — matplotlib turns those numbers into a visible image
plt.figure(figsize=(3, 3))
plt.imshow(tiny_image, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=255)
plt.title("Our 5×5 image")
plt.axis('off')
plt.show()
Output:



A dark 5×5 square with a bright centre pixel — created entirely from numbers


You are looking at a dark square with a bright centre. That image does not come from a camera. It comes from the array of numbers we typed. The grid of numbers is the image — there is nothing else behind it.

title: What is a Picture?
section: Colour images — three grids, not one
text:
Colour images — three grids, not one
A grayscale image is one grid of numbers. A colour image is three grids stacked together.
Each grid is called a channel. The three channels in a standard colour image represent:

R — how much Red is in each pixel (0 to 255)
G — how much Green is in each pixel (0 to 255)
B — how much Blue is in each pixel (0 to 255)

Together, RGB can represent approximately 16.7 million colours (256 × 256 × 256).
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Create a 100x100 colour image — manually set each channel
colour_image = np.zeros((100, 100, 3), dtype=np.uint8)
colour_image[:, :, 0] = 200   # Red channel   — strong
colour_image[:, :, 1] = 80    # Green channel — medium
colour_image[:, :, 2] = 20    # Blue channel  — weak

print("Shape:", colour_image.shape)
print("Red channel — sample values:", colour_image[0, :5, 0])
print("Green channel — sample values:", colour_image[0, :5, 1])
print("Blue channel — sample values:", colour_image[0, :5, 2])
Output:
Shape: (100, 100, 3)
Red channel — sample values:   [200 200 200 200 200]
Green channel — sample values: [ 80  80  80  80  80]
Blue channel — sample values:  [ 20  20  20  20  20]
# Plot the image and its three channels separately
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(14, 4))

axes[0].imshow(colour_image)
axes[0].set_title("Original colour")
axes[0].axis('off')

names = ['Red channel', 'Green channel', 'Blue channel']
cmaps = ['Reds', 'Greens', 'Blues']
for i in range(3):
    axes[i+1].imshow(colour_image[:, :, i], cmap=cmaps[i], vmin=0, vmax=255)
    axes[i+1].set_title(names[i])
    axes[i+1].axis('off')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Output:



The colour image split into its three individual channels — R, G, and B


Notice the shape — (100, 100, 3). This tells you everything: height 100, width 100, and 3 channels. That third dimension is where colour lives.






Important



Channels are not always 3. In the pixel explorer above you saw 3 channels — R, G, and B. But this number changes depending on the colour scheme used. An image can have 1 channel (Grayscale), 3 channels (RGB, BGR, or HSV), or 4 channels (RGBA — where the 4th channel stores transparency, like a PNG with a transparent background). We cover all of this properly in the next post.

title: What is a Picture?
section: What the shape tells you
text:
What the shape tells you
The shape of an image array is the single most important thing to check every time you load an image.
import cv2

img = cv2.imread('your_image.jpg')
print(img.shape)
# (480, 640, 3)
#    ^    ^   ^
#    |    |   +-- channels  (3 = colour,  1 = grayscale)
#    |    +------- width in pixels
#    +------------- height in pixels
Output:
(480, 640, 3)
Memory consumed by one image = height × width × channels × bytes per pixel
For a standard colour image: 480 × 640 × 3 × 1 byte = 921,600 bytes ≈ 0.9 MB
This is why batches of images consume so much memory during training. A batch of 32 images at 224×224 pixels = 32 × 224 × 224 × 3 = 48,365,568 bytes ≈ 46 MB. Per batch. Just for the images alone.

title: What is a Picture?
section: One important warning about OpenCV
text:
One important warning about OpenCV
OpenCV loads images in BGR order, not RGB.
import cv2
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

img_bgr = cv2.imread('your_image.jpg')      # OpenCV loads as BGR
img_rgb = cv2.cvtColor(img_bgr, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)  # convert to RGB

# Displayed with matplotlib — wrong colours (blue and red swapped)
plt.imshow(img_bgr)
plt.title("BGR — colours look wrong")
plt.show()

# Correct colours
plt.imshow(img_rgb)
plt.title("RGB — correct")
plt.show()
Output:



Same image displayed as BGR (wrong colours) and RGB (correct colours)


Blue and red channels are swapped. OpenCV does this for historical reasons. Just remember the rule — OpenCV = BGR. Everything else = RGB. Always convert before displaying.

title: What is a Picture?
section: What you should now be able to say
text:
What you should now be able to say
Before this post, you knew an image was “a file you open with OpenCV.”
Now you know:

An image is a rectangular grid of numbers — nothing more
Each number is a pixel value between 0 and 255
Grayscale images have 1 channel, colour images have 3 (or sometimes 4)
The shape of an image array is (height, width, channels) — height always first
OpenCV loads in BGR, not RGB — always convert before displaying
Memory = height × width × channels — and this adds up fast

This is the foundation. Every algorithm in computer vision — convolutions, feature detection, object detection, segmentation — operates on this grid of numbers. The better you understand the grid, the better you will understand everything built on top of it.

Next in First Principles → Why Does Color Space Matter? — RGB, BGR, HSV, LAB, Grayscale — what each one actually represents and when using the wrong one silently ruins your results.
